deck_meta.title: What 81,000 People Told Us About the Economics of AI
deck_meta.subtitle: Worker Sentiment, Job Threat, and Productivity Gains from the Anthropic Economic Index Survey
deck_meta.deck_audience: Enterprise leaders, HR & workforce strategists, AI policy teams

slides.title: Economics of AI: What 81,000 Users Said
slides.subtitle: Direct evidence linking AI exposure, productivity, and job-displacement fear
slides.footer_meta: Anthropic · Massenkoff & Huang · 2026 · 81K Claude users survey

slides.title: Why This Survey Matters
slides.key_message: First large-scale qualitative bridge from observed AI usage to what users actually feel.

points: Economic Index: what tasks Claude performs
Observed exposure: % of a job's tasks Claude touches
Tells us what — but not how users feel about it
No direct link between usage and sentiment
81,000 self-selected Claude users
Open-ended interview inside Claude.ai
Claude-powered classifiers extract sentiment, job, career stage
Connects observed-exposure data to perceived job threat

items.label: Key Findings & Trends
items.sub: Three headline patterns from the survey

items.label: Themes
items.sub: Who fears AI · who benefits from AI

slides.title: 01  Key Findings & Trends
preview: High AND low wage gain most

slides.title: The Headline Numbers
slides.key_message: Productivity gains are real and large — but anxiety scales with exposure.

stats.label: Voice concern about job displacement
stats.context: 20% of respondents directly expressed worry about losing their role

stats.label: Top-25% exposure vs bottom-25% mentioning job threat
stats.context: +1.3 percentage points in perceived threat per 10pt increase in exposure

stats.label: Cite scope gains · speed gains
stats.context: Scope = 'I'm a non-tech guy but now I'm a full-stack developer'

slides.narrative: Two stories sit on top of each other: AI is making people meaningfully more productive — and the same people, especially those most exposed, are increasingly nervous about what comes next.

slides.title: Thesis: People's Intuitions Match the Usage Data
slides.primary_quote: People's intuitions track the usage data: they worry most about AI's effect in the jobs where we observe Claude doing the most work.
slides.secondary_quote: Well like anyone who has a white collar job these days I'm 100% concerned, pretty much 24/7 concerned about losing my job eventually to A.I.
slides.implication: Workers are not naïve. They can feel the diffusion of AI in their own occupations long before formal labor statistics show displacement. The fear is rational — and concentrated where Claude is most active.

slides.title: Three Patterns Behind the Headline Numbers
slides.key_message: Productivity, threat perception, and benefit flow each follow distinct distributions.

columns.body: The more AI does in your occupation, the more you fear displacement. Linear, measurable, and tracks observed exposure.
columns.evidence: Every 10pt increase in observed exposure = +1.3pt in perceived job threat. Top quartile mentions worry 3× as often as bottom quartile.

columns.body: Highest- and lowest-wage occupations report the largest productivity gains. Middle-wage groups gain the least.
columns.evidence: Software developers report top gains — but so do customer service reps and delivery drivers building side businesses.

columns.body: Early-career workers are simultaneously more anxious and less confident they personally benefit.
columns.evidence: Only 60% of early-career say they personally benefit, vs 80% of senior professionals.

slides.title: Theme A — Scope and Speed, Not Just Speed
slides.key_message: Most users describe AI unlocking new abilities, not just faster execution.

points: "I'm a non-tech guy but now I'm a full-stack developer"
Customer service rep starts e-commerce business
Landscaper builds music application
AI lets users attempt tasks they couldn't before
"15 minutes that used to take 2 hours" — accountant
Faster code reviews, contract checks, paperwork
Quality gains via more thorough verification
Cost gains: replace work that exceeded budget

callout.label: Why this matters
callout.text: AI is not just an efficiency tool — for many users, it is an access tool. The distinction shapes who gains and how.

slides.title: Theme B — Speedup and Anxiety Form a U-Shape
slides.key_message: Both extremes — being slowed down and being sped up the most — correlate with the highest job threat.

points: Creative workers find AI stifling, rigid
Fear AI diffusion into their field anyway
Examples: fine artists, certain writers
Highest threat perception in the U
Largest measured productivity gains
Highest threat perception above baseline
Logic: if my tasks shrink rapidly, what's left?
Speed gain ≠ job security

callout.label: Strategic implication
callout.text: The narrative "AI just makes good workers more productive" misses half the story. The most-empowered users are also the most worried.

slides.source_note: Figure 6 — U-shaped relationship between speedup level and perceived job threat

slides.title: Strategic Insight: Two Long-Term Risks
slides.key_message: Today's positive sentiment hides structural questions about who actually captures the gains.

cards.body: Most workers say benefits flow to themselves — but a meaningful share report employers extracting the gains.
cards.evidence: 10% of respondents who named a beneficiary said their employer or clients were the ones getting more work from them.
cards.implication: → Productivity gain ≠ wage gain. Without bargaining power, the surplus accrues to firms, not workers.

cards.body: The cohort with the most anxiety is also the cohort least confident they personally benefit — a structural mismatch.
cards.evidence: Only 60% of early-career workers see themselves benefiting (vs 80% of senior). Hiring of recent grads is already slowing in the U.S.
cards.implication: → The traditional 'climb the ladder' career path may be eroding from the bottom rungs first.

slides.title: 05  Recommended Actions
slides.kicker: What enterprise leaders should do now

slides.title: Five Moves for Enterprise Leaders
slides.key_message: Workers' fear of AI is rational and predictable — and you can act on it.

actions.what: Build your own observed-exposure map: % of each role's tasks done by AI today.
actions.outcome: Identify which teams will feel threatened first; prioritize change management there.

actions.header: Focus on Scope, Not Just Speed
actions.what: Position AI as an access tool — enabling new tasks — rather than a labor-cost cutter.
actions.outcome: Aligns with the 48% who already frame AI as scope-expansion; reduces displacement framing.

actions.what: Create AI-augmented apprenticeship programs that explicitly protect early-career talent development.
actions.outcome: Counters the early-career squeeze; preserves the future senior pipeline.

actions.what: Explicit gain-sharing schemes — bonuses, equity, or time-off — tied to measurable AI-driven productivity wins.
actions.outcome: Closes the 'employer captures the gain' gap; converts anxiety into engagement.

actions.header: Talk to the Anxious Top Performers
actions.what: Don't assume highest-speedup workers feel safest. Run sentiment listening explicitly with them.
actions.outcome: Heads off attrition in your highest-leverage staff; turns U-shape insight into retention.

slides.title: The Big Picture
slides.key_message: AI is empowering and unsettling its users at the same time — and the two feelings live in the same people.

pillars.body: The first large-scale qualitative data linking observed AI usage to user sentiment. Productivity gains are real, broad-based, and skewed bimodally across the wage distribution.
Worker anxiety is not paranoia — it tracks measurable exposure. The most-empowered users are simultaneously the most worried. Career stage shapes who benefits.
Beneficiary capture (who keeps the surplus) and the early-career squeeze (who gets in) are the two structural risks behind the positive top-line numbers.

slides.closing_line: The opportunity is not just to deploy AI — it is to do so in a way that lets the workforce share both the upside and the certainty.

items: Massenkoff, M. & Huang, S. (2026). What 81,000 people told us about the economics of AI. Anthropic Research / Economic Index.
Companion: Anthropic Economic Index March 2026 Report.
Sample: 81,000 self-selected Claude.ai personal-account users (acknowledgements cite 80,508).
Format: Open-ended interview embedded in Anthropic Interviewer inside Claude.ai.
Inference: Claude-powered classifiers extract occupation, career stage, productivity, threat, beneficiary.
5.1 mean productivity rating — §Who benefits from AI?
1 in 5 voice job-loss concern — §Who worries about job displacement?
U-shape between speedup and threat — Figure 6
